drop_dates.2025-10-31: Halloween covers set. The band played someone else's record front to back, and those titles are not things they might play next week -- left in, they poison the vocabulary for every ordinary show. Dropping the date loses the two real songs in the encore, and that is the right trade.

example2024-06-14.why: The uploader typed the wrong year. Their own description says 'June 14 2025', the artwork is named for the 2025 run, and it was uploaded three weeks after the 2025 show. Believing the metadata invents a show that never happened AND buries half of a real one, which nothing downstream can detect: a phantom show looks exactly like a real one nobody else taped.

junk_patterns.why: A band this one covers. The taper writes the artist where the song title belongs, so without this the covered ARTIST enters the vocabulary as a song. Note it is a drop, not a tag: nothing was performed under this name, the parser just found a credit.

gear_patterns.why: A recorder this scene uses, written into the lineage preamble. Every taper scene has its own shorthand and setlistkit ships only the gear words everyone writes, so the local dialect goes here.

acoustic_patterns.why: This band's billing when the SAME five play an unplugged night. It is tagged and not refused because the band is all there: the songs are real evidence about what this band plays, so the row belongs in the rotation model and only the length statistics leave it out -- a song that runs 18 minutes electric runs 6 unplugged, and pooling the two averages distributions that have nothing to do with each other. Matched on the BRAND name and never on the bare word 'acoustic', because tapers list their gear in the description and 'percussion, flute, acoustic guitar' is not an acoustic show.

side_project_patterns.why: Two of the five under a related name, which is not the same act however similar the billing looks. Fifteen songs by a duo are not fifteen plays by the band, so this REFUSES the tape rather than tagging it: left in, those songs enter the rotation model and the base rates as ordinary plays. This is the opposite of acoustic_patterns above and the difference is only ever who was on stage. Matched against the tape's whole text, not its title, because a billing usually hides in the description while the title reads like any other show.
